chemicals.chemical_formula: Not provided in the given sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: The overall toxicity level of the Oyster Plant is considered mild1. The irritant sap is distributed throughout all parts of the plant, but is specifically noted as being contained within the leaves
chemicals.description: in cats: The sap acts as a direct physical and chemical irritant. When a cat bites or chews the plant, the sap is released, causing immediate irritation to the oral mucous membranes and gastrointestinal tract, which leads to drooling and vomiting1. Additionally, it acts as a contact irritant; if the sap gets onto the cat's fur or skin, it can occasionally cause localized skin irritation or rash
chemicals.name: The plant's sap contains irritating compounds, specifically oxalates (calcium oxalate crystals) along with other unspecified irritants

description: Oyster Plant (Tradescantia spathacea), also known as Moses-in-the-Cradle or Boat Lily, is a common houseplant and garden ornamental that is mildly toxic to cats. It contains calcium oxalate crystals and dermatitis-causing compounds in its sap. Skin contact with the plant or ingestion can both cause reactions.
name: Oyster Plant
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: calcium oxalate crystals and irritant compounds in the sap; the vivid purple undersides of leaves contain higher concentrations
Mechanism: calcium oxalate crystals cause mechanical irritation to the oral and GI mucosa; sap contact causes contact dermatitis
Symptoms: drooling, vomiting, diarrhea; skin or eye contact with sap may cause redness, irritation, or rash
The ornamental purple-and-green leaves make this a popular houseplant — keep it out of cat reach
Symptoms are usually mild; rinse any sap off skin with water and consult your vet if GI symptoms persist

symptoms.name: Drooling (hypersalivation) and vomiting
symptoms.notes: If your cat ingests this plant, you should gently wipe out their mouth to remove any residual irritant sap and offer water1. You should monitor them for persistent vomiting and consult a veterinarian if symptoms worsen or do not resolve1. 2. Skin Irritation
symptoms.onset: The sources do not specify an exact timeframe, but because it is an immediate physical irritant,

symptoms.name: Skin irritation or rash
symptoms.notes: This is an occasional symptom that happens when the cat's skin or fur comes into contact with the sap1. It is recommended to wipe the cat's paws to remove the sap, and to consult a vet if the rash persists
symptoms.onset: The sources do not specify an exact timeframe,

treatments.description: Gently wipe out the cat's mouth and thoroughly clean its paws to remove any remaining toxic, irritant sap
treatments.name: Oral and Paw Decontamination
treatments.notes: This is an urgent at-home first aid step to stop the sap from causing further irritation to the skin or gastrointestinal tract1. Because the plant's toxicity is mild, this is often the only intervention required. 2. Observation and Monitoring

treatments.description: Keep a close eye on the cat for a period of time after exposure, watching for signs of mild gastrointestinal upset (drooling or vomiting) or skin redness/rash
treatments.name: Symptomatic Monitoring
treatments.notes: If the vomiting is persistent, or if the skin rash worsens and does not resolve on its own, it is time to consult a veterinarian for professional guidance1. 3. Veterinary Symptomatic Support (If Symptoms Worsen)

treatments.description: If the cat's symptoms escalate, a veterinarian will assess the animal and provide targeted medical relief, which may include anti-emetic medications to stop persistent vomiting or treatments to soothe severe skin irritation.
treatments.name: Veterinary Symptomatic Care
treatments.notes: Because Oyster Plant ingestion typically only results in mild, self-limiting symptoms, this step is only necessary for cats that have an unusually severe reaction or do not recover quickly
